```
A string of characters representing an Integer in base-10 (decimal), shall contain only the characters 0 - 9, with an optional leading "+" or "-". It may be padded with leading and/or trailing spaces. Embedded spaces are not allowed.
The integer, n, represented shall be in the range:
-231<= n <= (231-1).
```

The nomenclature here is tricky, because "relation" actually has two meanings in Juju.

The specific, named endpoints as defined in `metadata.yaml` are relations, with each relation having an interface protocol that it uses.  This is what each instance of the interface layer's class represents.

The actual connection, from one named endpoint in one charm to a named endpoint in another charm is also called a relation.  This is what each item in the `self.applications` collection represents.

I suppose the uninstantiated class represents the interface protocol, but the instance of that class represents the relation endpoint.  I could get on board with calling the base class `Interface`, `RelationInterface`, or `RelationEndpoint` rather than `SimpleRelation`.  Maybe we go wtih `Interface` and change the collection to `self.relations` and use "relation endpoint" to describe the name defined in `metadata.yaml`?

This file is 100% new code; most of the "deleted" code in the diff was actually just moved to another file. `CacheWithTimeout` and `TieredCache` were moved verbatim to `cache_helpers.py` and `QuickCache` and `SkippableQuickCache` were merged into a single class and moved to `quickcache_helper.py`.

Where we previously exposed a `quickcache` decorator-maker function directly, we now expose `get_quickcache` which you can call to configure your decorator-maker. I arranged it so that these `QuickCache` objects, which are spruced-up `namedtuples`, can be used to generate a copy of themselves with modified settings by calling `but_with` (which is just an alias for `namedtuple`'s `._replace`). `get_quickcache` is then actually an alias for a preconfigured `QuickCache` instance's `but_with` method, i.e. "take this default object and replace values as you like".

It's a little roundabout, but was actually super helpful in making sure that the settings can be overridden at any point from your initial `quickcache` configuration to when you're using it as a decorator-make, and that the behavior is the same no matter where it's set. As you can see, the `__call__` method above (which is what's called when you do `@quickcache(...)`) first uses `.but_with` on the arguments you pass, which makes sure they're treated the same as if they were set on the object in the first place. (Like I said, it's a bit roundabout, but when I was toying around with other ways of doing this, it was by far the logically simplest/easiest to reason with while getting the desired behavior.)

Imagine you want to create an `archive.tar.gz` that looks like:

```
./bin/foo.pex
./bin/bar.pex
./bin/baz.jar
./etc/foo.ini
./etc/bar.cfg
./etc/baz.xml
```

Where it has multiple binaries, multiple "extra files", etc. That feels like something Pants could bundle up for you, rather than having the script multiple invocations of Pants to make all the artifacts, and to archive things up at the end.

This "fancy bundling" is not included in this change, but directionally I think the additional flexibility would be really valuable for users.

> grabbed the date formatting from openssl x509 -inform pem -noout -text -in

Huh! That's wacky of the OpenSSL project...

> what's a possible use case for machine-readable?

People writing their own renewal scripts (maybe because they have criteria or associated actions that aren't well-covered by the behavior of `certbot renew`) or writing pre- and post-hooks for renewal that need to know more about what certificates exist, again because they want to take some action based on what happens or what exists. (For example, maybe something about notification to a human administrator, or something about synchronization between machines in a cluster -- like rsyncing only the certificates that changed in the course of the renewal process, perhaps?)

> can we default to human-readable, and then also have a machine-readable flag?

Yep, I think that would be a good choice.

> I really like the (89 days from now / EXPIRED) idea!

There are probably a lot of library approaches to doing this -- for example if you have a pair of `datetime` objects you can subtract them and then refer to the `.days` attribute of the resulting `timedelta` objects, but if you can get the expiry time as Unix time, you can say something like

```
diff = expiry - time.now()
if diff <= 0:
    return "EXPIRED"
elif diff < 86400:
    return "expiring today"
elif diff < 86400*2:
    return "1 day"
else:
    return "{0} days".format(diff % 86400)
```
